titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: Sanitarium of the West. Rambles About San Francisco and Down the Pacific Coast. Stories of Spanish Fathers. Barbara, the Saint--The Lost Woman of San Nicolas--Remains of the Old Museum--Traces of the Mound Builders--Golden Gate.

head.title: Rambles About San Francisco and Down the Pacific Coast.
STORIES OF SPANISH FATHERS
Barbara, the Saint—The Lost Woman of San Nicolas—Remains of the Old Museum—Traces of the Mound Builders—Golden Gate.

div.byline: Correspondence of the Democrat and Chronicle.
div.p: The early Spanish fathers of the Pacific coast gave the missions [sic] towns they founded the names and patronage of particular saints that had become worthy of a place in their calendar. San Francisco was called in honor of Saint Francis, San Pedro from Saint Peter, Santa Barbara, which is popularly known as the sanitarium of the Pacific coast, by reason of the medicinal properties in its climate, bears the name of Barbara, the saint. She was an only child, of great beauty and intelligence. Descorus, her father, was an idolater, of noble birth and great wealth. Descorus shut her up in a tower to monopolize her affections. He gave her musical instruments and tutors, and fitted up her apartments gorgeously. Through a tract, she learned of Origen, with whom she found means to communicate and by whom she was converted and baptised. Her father wanted her to marry a distinguished man of the time, but Barbara opposed it so persistently that he gave it up for a time. On his subsequent return from a long journey, he repaired to the tower and asked his daughter if she would relent and marry the man, when she gave him the touching reply that her love for him would not permit her to leave him in his old age for a husband. The answer so pleased the idolatrous parent that he released her from the tower and took her home. On entering her father's house and finding it filled with idols, she inquired, "Of what use are these ridiculous puppets!" Descorus upbraided her for her want of respect for the gods, and gave her up to punishment for her Christianity. The judge tried to shake her faith in the religion of Jesus, but she clung firmly to a faith that was [has] taught so many how to live and how to die. Her father asked to be her executioner, and as soon as this unnatural and inhumane parent had struck off her head he fell dead at her feet. Such is the affectious story of Barbara, a martyr of hystory [history] who has given her name and memory to one of the most beautiful cities on this coast. She suffered martyrdom in the reign of Maximus I. She is a star of the first magnitude in the constellation of saints, and is honored with particular devotion in the Latin, Greek, Muscovite and Syriac calendar.

Nature has made Santa Barbara beautiful for situation, as the person, life and character of the saint were whose name it bears. A combination of ocean, bay, mountain, vale, hill, climate and sky contributes to this. The Pacific coast which trends from the northwest to the southeast, makes an abrupt turn at Point Conception, and runs directly east one hundred miles, so that Santa Barbara sets [sits] a queen on that shore with her feet in the bay and her face to the south and the ocean. The Santa Ynez mountains, 3,500 feet high, are parallel to that shore, a few miles north of it, standing like a long row of sentinels to watch and defend it. The long narrow valley between them and the sea is fifty miles long and five wide, protected from the cold winds of the north by the mountains, and from the damp fogs and winds of the ocean by the mountainous islands of the shore. In that favored and protected spot of earth, known as Santa Barbara valley the climate has an average of 54° in winter, 60° in spring, 68° in the summer, all the seasons of the year being one even-tempered sweetness, long drawn out. It is a climate that will beat Mentone in the south of France, or Venice, "the pride of the sea," or Greece, the summer land of eloquence, poetry and song. Santa Barbara, a town of 5,000 inhabitants, with its palatial residences, adobe houses, masts of trade, cultured inhabitants, air redolent of perfume, and the everlasting bloom of its roses, lies between the mountains on the one side and the Mega on the other, which intervenes between it and the sea.

lg.l: "In the fairest of valleys, on an evergreen shore,
By mountains walled in and an ocean before,
With her brow on the hills and her feet to the sea;
Santa Barbara stands—the queen that's to be.
'Tis the land of all lands where Flora in pride
Each month of the year has a rose by her side.
Flowers, bright-hued, most fragrant and rare,
Like rainbows entwining her, perfuming the air."

div.p: That which adds health to the climate and gives interest to the scenery of Santa Barbara are the islands that rest on the placid bosom of the Pacific, over which nature has thrown a garment of enchantment. One never wearies looking at their smoky, hazy outlines. They are like so many angelic [countenances?] and watch over the interests of [?], amusement and commerce. The first in order is the Santa Rosa island, which is next to the largest. Santa Cruz island is the largest, having in it 60,000 acres. It is twenty-seven miles from the shore, and upon it are 50,000 sheep. It is particularly adapted to sheep raising as it is free from the [?] weed, which is such a detriment to [w]ool growing. There they are allowed to [ro]am at will and are rounded up twice a year [at] shearing time. Santa Cruz has a very [go]od harbor in which ships may anchor safely in a storm. The Anna Capas are [th]ree in number—a trio sisterhood of islands. [San] Miguel and San[ta] Catalina are among [these] emerald jewels of the Pacific ocean. [Seve]nty miles from the shore is San Nicolas island, the sixth of the group that cluster [around] the Bay of Santa Barbara. It is [celeb]rated for having been the residence for [eighte]en years of "The lost woman of San Nicolas." As her name is famous, and as [her sto]ry has been told in romance, I will give the bare facts about her as I found them to be without any added ornament, and leave the fiction to the pen of the novelist.

The island of San Nicolas was once densely populated. This is attested by the skulls and bones and other evidences of human abode that are found there. The Alaska Indians frequently visited the island to hunt for the furs of the other [otter]. They took their women with them on these hunts, and on returning after the hunt was over, they left them on the island to their fate. By order of the government in 1835, the Indians were all removed from the island. Just before they were to embark, and carry away every human inhabitant, as it was supposed, a white Indian woman begged for the privilege of going ashore for her child, which she had accidentally left. She was gone a long time in a fruitless search for her offspring. The wind blew a gale, the water was shallow, there was no one who was seeking the lost one. They set sail, intending to return for the lost woman of San Nicolas, but the boat becoming unseaworthy, she was left to her fate. It was generally known that there was a woman abandoned on the island, but no attempt was made for her deliverance. As the world soon forgets, so the woman of San Nicolas was forgotten as the years passed away. After a lapse of sixteen years, a man who landed on the island found that it was inhabited by some one. The footprints of a human being sunk deep in the ground that was dry and hard, were seen. The hardness of the ground was an indication that they had been made a long time. From their diminutive size they evidently belonged to a woman. Circular inclosures, made of brush, six or seven feet through were seen, where someone had lived. Near the huts were sticks driven in the ground and dried blubber hanging on them, thus prepared for food.

p.span: In 1852 San Nicolas was visited again, and another hunt was made for the long lost woman. The huts were seen that had been seen before, but the old blubber had been taken away, and new food of the kind hung in its place. Wild dogs were found which ran away at the approach of civilized man. In a basket covered with seal skins that they found in a bush-heap was a dress made of shag skins, cut in square pieces, a rope made of sinew, and several smaller articles, such as fish hooks and bone needles. The discoveries [discoverers] of these proofs that there lived on that island a human being, scattered them on the ground, and on their return they found them gathered together and placed in the basket again.

div.p: The Catholic fathers of the "Old mission," the foremost in every good word and work, offered a reward to anyone who would find the lost woman, and a third effort was made in 1853 to discover the prodigal about whom there was such a mystery, and in whom the world of this coast that had forgotten, was beginning to take an interest. Fresh tracks of the woman were found. They belonged to very small feet and were like those that were seen in the first attempt to find the woman. Huts made of whale ribs and covered with brush were discovered. At length an object was seen in the distance that appeared to be moving. Going towards it, they saw the woman that for eighteen years had reigned queen of San Nicolas, her subjects being the wild animals, her robe a dress of bird skins she had made, and her throne the hut she had bullt [sic], she was seated in an inclosure so that only her head and shoulders could be seen. Two or three dogs were about her that disappeared at the command of their sovereign. She was seated on some grass that covered the ground and served her as a bed. Her dress was neatly made of bird skins extending to her ankles, the feather[s] of which pointed downward. Her head covered with a thick mat of yellowish brown hair, and her shoulders rounded in their fullness, were bare. She was occupied in stripping blubber from a piece of sealskin which she held across her knee, and using for that purpose a rude knife made of an iron hook. In her hut was a fire beside which she sat talking to herself, and occasionally shading her eyes with her hand, looked at the men that were in search of her. She received them with much courtesy, bowing and smiling and showing that this last child of nature had much of the politeness of a true woman. She took some roots from a bag, roasted them in the fire and gave them to the men to eat. She gathered up all her food which was her chief concern owing to the famines she had experienced, washed herself at the spring, and followed the men cheerfully to the boat. On being taken to Santa Barbara she manifested much delight on seeing the sights of civilization. She became a universal favorite and was presented with many gifts. Passengers on the Panama steamer that landed there, all wanted to see the lost woman of San Nicolas. She related by signs her experience on the island, as she spoke a language that none of the Indians knew, and none of the Spanish fathers, versed in Indian dialects, could understand. Civilization which prolongs the life of some, shortened that of the woman of San Nicolas. An excessive love of fruit led to her death in Santa Barbara at the age of 50. She was buried with the honors of the Catholic church by the Franciscan Fathers, and her dress and trinkets were sent to Rome and placed in that receptacle of the world's curiosities.

p.span: The story of the lost woman of San Nicolas will always remain a wonderment. Her ingenuity is seen in the pails she made out of grass that could hold water, by smearing them [with] melted asphaltum, and in her bird-skin dresses, her bone needles, her knife of iron hoop, and the language she invented and spoke. It is inexplicable to all who have learned her story how a woman could live eighteen years on an island around which the waves were beating an inhospitable requiem to hope, getting her food and cooking it without even those facilities which are common to the savage, and making her dresses neatly without the aid of needles except what she carved out of bone.

She laid aside one dress for eighteen years to be worn when the white men should come of which she had a presentiment. She built her own house, protected herself from the cold, gained supreme control over the wild beasts about her, and preserved her love for men and women for well nigh two decades of human life alone in the wild. With no previous knowledge of civilization, solitude appeared to have educated her for it. An ordinary woman would have perished with dispondency [sic] in such a desolate, hungry, naked, shelterless outlook. She is, and will remain to the last event of recorded time, an example to the world how the genius of a woman can brighten and what her powers of cheerful endurance can be, on an inhabitable shore, without friends, or tools, or shelter, or clothes, or food, or encouragement. O heroism, thy name is woman.

The Dominican friars were the forerunners of civilization on this coast, and early recognized the advantages and beauties of Santa Barbara, and established a mission there on a commanding eminence. Its white towers served as a landmark far out to sea, and may be seen glimmering in the sunlight long before any signs of the town come to view. The mission building is 160 feet in length and 30 feet wide with walls six feet thick, the stone hewn out of solid rock. Two solid stone towers run up in front to the height of 57 feet through which winding stairways ascend to the top, and on which melodious chimes of bells have been ringing out tidings of the Catholic faith since the days of the Indians and for one hundred years. They look as dilapidated and as ancient as the bell in Independence Hall that rung our nation into being and some of them have had to be recast. A wing of the building extends 220 feet long and 100 feet wide. This far-famed "Old Mission," the pride of Protestant and Catholic alike, was twenty years in building and is well nigh 100 years old. That event is to be celebrated with centennial exercises next year. In it are wooden wardrobes, hewn out of the wood by the Indians, containing the vestments of the priests brought from Spain, old and costly, the statues of Saints Barbara and Francil [Francis], the latter being the patron and saint of the Franciscan order, a garden of choice trees, plants and flowers where a woman is never allowed to enter, the vaults where the fathers and the Franciscan brothers are buried, the tomb of the first Catholic bishop of California, ancient pottery dug out of the tombs of the Indians and the mound builders, the old Spanish statues of Jesus with representations of blood streaming from side, hands and feet. Everything in and around the Old Mission bears the evidence of age. Its furnishings were transported from Spain under the auspices of its being it was built. The old mill, gone to decay, the adobe houses, crumbled to ruins, the Indian burial ground in which the Indians are buried in trenches, the brick tiled roofs, the excavated mines in which the Indians worked, all cluster around this ancient edifice, show the antiquity of this mission, and the generations that have come and gone since its foundations were laid. I found there six Franciscan friars, besides the fathers, wearing the color and dress of that order—gray gowns, cowls, cables and sandals, living on the plainest food, and sworn to a life of celi[b]acy, chastity, and poverty. They eschew the loveliness of womanhood and the pleasures and gaities of life, wear their sandals and their gray, and shut themselves up in their cells—those living tombs—from a world which attracts only to show its emptiness, and elevates only to depress. If the happiness of the soul in future life is proportionate to the abstemiousness of the body in this, what then must be the rewards in store for the Franciscan brotherhood!

The mounds of the mound builders dot this coast all along from Mount Shasta to San Diego. They are especially numerous in Santa Barbara, and their vessels and implements of war and industry are in the homes, curiosity shops and marts of trade of its citizens. Their mounds, telling in silence and yet most eloquently of the pre-historic existence of races in the long-gone-by, rise in Montecito, in Carpenteria, among the Santa Ynez mountains, and from Point Conception to the Meza. At Point Rancon [sic] several hundred acres are covered at a depth of from two to fifteen feet with the debris of what was once a large populatoin. Relics of all kinds are exhumed—mortars, pestles, hammers, knives, arrow and lance heads, utensils of various kinds, all laboriously carved from the hardest stone. At one place more than ten tons of relics were dug up. The islands already spoken of are rich in these remains, and large quantities of them have been dug out of their sea-girt homes, where they have rested for thousands of years, and shipped away to foreign museums and colleges, to awaken aspirations that can never be gratified, in students of other lands, to know who, what, where and when were the Mound Builders.

div.p: The Santa Barbarians are a cultured, refined, amusement-loving people. It appears that the best mannered and educated of eastern circles of society, have floated on the tidal wave that had broken on that shore, and, in that little [?] between the mountains, the islands and the sea—a sweet valley of rest have settled down to breath[e] in its fragrant air, bathe in its ocean tides, and bask in its perennial sunshine. I have found in no other community of the same size, not the center of great schools so many polite, well-informed man and women, and free public library in a building that is an imposing monument to the belevolence, of 4,000 volumes, containing departments of shells, minerals and natural history, founded by the munificence of a Vale, a Dominick, a Calkins and others, is proof of the culture of the place. In the homes of the people I found libraries of classical, scientific, historical and periodical lore, shells from all seas properly arranged and classified, sea mosses and ferns arranged in like manner and pressed into ponderous volumes, geological specimens carefully prepared over their Latin names and placed in costly cabinets, birds stuffed in glass coverings, from the gay bird of paradise to the unpretentious thrush of the wild wood. The love of amusement among the Santa Barbarians is equal to their love of learning. It finds expression in the various walks and drives about the city. Horseback riding is common and popular, and ladies attain excellence in equestrianship. Carriage drives are numerous and excellent, amid scenery that is unparalleled, under skies that are cloudless, and in air that is balm-ladened [balm-laden]. The beach of the ocean, hard, smooth and dry, is a good road for a ride or drive and well used. The canons of the mountains, wild, weird and picturesque, are used for recreations of that character. The foot paths over the hills and up the mountains are good places for the exercise of pedestrianship, the gaining of health and the enjoyment of pleasure.

The productions of Santa Barbara are its pride as well as its climate and scenery. It is a land where there is a scant rain fall—no irrigation, and yet the cereals and vegetables and nuts of all kinds, of both temperate and tropical regions, grow there. The lima bean, the orange, the lemon, the prune, the rubber tree, the cork in its lightness and the aloe in its bitterness, all fructify in that soil. Tomatoes ripen the winter through. That is the only place on earth where I have found a rose fair once a year. A Santa Barbara pumpkin grew last year to the size of 800 pounds. The grape is the being of fruit in that latitude in size and sweetness. Santa Barbara is the home of "the Centennial grape vine," a section of which was taken to the Centenial at Philadelphia. It covers a place twenty-five feet long and twenty wide. Who has not heard of the twig of grape that a lover gave to the girl of his choice to whip her horse with on her homeward way, and in the commemoration of the event, she planted it, which afterwards became more distinguished for its fruitage than any other vine in Santa Barbara county?

The distance by ocean from Santa Barbara to this city is 288 miles, and the time in making it is two nights and a day. The Santa Rosa steamer—a floating palace—was my conveyance home from that city to this. She is 348 feet long and 24 feet depth of hold, and has a compound engine of 3,000 horse-power, two complete iron decks going the whole length of the boat, nine water-tight bulk-heads (which admit of carrying 1,500 tons of water ballast), fifty-eight state-rooms, 188 berths, six life-boats, eight metal life-rafts, four anchors, 450 cork life-preservers, patent logue to measure ahead, 400 electric lights of 6,400 candle power. Each state-room is lighted with electricity and heated with steam. The steamer has a gross tonage of 3,000 tons, and cost $600,000. The cabin is vulcanized cherry and mahogany, and upholstered throughout with silk plush and damask imported from France especially for the boat. It requires six carcasses of beef, twelve sheep, six hogs, six dozen of poultry and 500 pounds of fish and game for passengers and crew in her rough trip of a thousand miles. She was built at Chester, Pa., is capable of carrying 375 passengers, and was brought around Cape Horn in February of last year by Captain Johnson, her gentlemanly commander. In the twilight of the morning we entered the Golden Gate, first called by that name in the "Geographical Memoirs of California" in 1848, expressive of the commerce that enters it and the gold within. Father Junipero entered in 1769, exclaiming, "there is the port, to which St. Francis has led us—blessed be his holy name," and the bay within was called San Francisco bay in honor of the saint. The golden gate is five miles long and from on to two miles wide, and is the long narrow entrance into San Francisco bay. That bay is seventy miles long, two-thirds of it being south of the entrance and the whole of it being at right angles to the entrance. On the right as you enter in, is Fort Point, garrisoned by United States soldiers and mounted with cannon. Its frowning guns are capable, on account of that advantageous position, of annihilating the navy of an enemy. Farther down the channel and at the right also is Black Point, the residence and headquarters of the commandant of the fort. Slcatraz [Alcatraz] island on the left, rising a black huge mass of rock above the bay, is a military prison where officers and soldiers from all parts of the United States are put for offenses against military discipline. Goat island is soon passed which belongs to the govenrnment. There are some of the water-works of the city. The railroad comapnies have had covitous [sic] eyes on Goat island for a long time. By building a pier to it they can then unite the commerce of land and sea, but the government cannot be bribed to sell it. The Santa Rosa lands at her pier and underneath the stars and stripes that there float over the gathered commerce of all nations, we are again at home, in this great city of the Pacific shore—San Francisco.

In 1847, this city had 459 inhabitants. In 1848 it was a cluster of houses among the sand hills, rimmed around by the bay like the edge of a saucer. Now it has reached the greatness of a population of 250,000, and is the financial, commercial, business, legal, intellectual, social, moral, religious and news center of the western side of this continent. "Rome sat upon her seven hills and ruled the world," but San Francisco sits upon more than twice that number and sways her scepter over a greater empire than that of the Romans.
